title: Computer Won't Turn On
cardText: Diagnose a desktop or laptop that does not power on.
Determine whether the problem is power delivery, battery, power supply, motherboard, or startup related.
Follow the checks in order before replacing hardware.
text: This guide is for computers that do not start when the power button is pressed.
Identify whether any signs of power are present.
Check external power sources before investigating internal hardware.
Escalate hardware faults when basic troubleshooting fails.

pressPowerButton.title: Press the power button
body: Press the power button normally.
Observe whether any lights, fan movement, sounds, or screen activity occur.
Any sign of activity helps identify where the startup process is failing.

checkPowerSource.title: Check power source
body: Confirm the wall outlet has power.
Try another device such as a lamp or phone charger.
If using a power board, ensure it is switched on.

restorePower.title: Restore electrical power
body: The outlet or power board is not providing power.
Restore power or move the computer to a working outlet before continuing.

checkPowerCable.title: Check power cable and adapter
body: Ensure the power cable is firmly connected.
For laptops, verify the charger is connected correctly.
Look for damaged cables, bent pins, or loose connectors.

replacePowerCable.title: Replace power cable or charger
body: A damaged power cable or charger can prevent the system from receiving power.
Replace the faulty component and test again.

desktopOrLaptop.title: Identify device type
body: Determine whether the affected device is a desktop computer or laptop.
Different power systems require different troubleshooting steps.

checkLaptopBattery.title: Check laptop charging indicators
body: Connect the charger and look for charging LEDs.
Leave the charger connected for at least 10 minutes.
A completely discharged battery may require time before startup.

attemptLaptopStart.title: Attempt startup while connected to power
body: Leave the charger connected.
Press the power button again.
Observe whether the laptop now powers on.

laptopPowerFault.title: Possible charger, battery, or motherboard fault
body: The laptop is not showing evidence of receiving power.
Test with a known-good charger if available.
If the problem remains, hardware repair may be required.

checkPsuSwitch.title: Check power supply switch
body: Ensure the switch on the back of the power supply is set to ON.
Some desktop power supplies also include a voltage selector switch which should not normally be changed.

enablePsu.title: Enable power supply
body: The power supply was switched off.
Turn it on and test the computer again.

checkMotherboardLed.title: Check motherboard standby power
body: If safe to open the case, look for a standby LED on the motherboard.
A standby LED usually indicates the motherboard is receiving power.

psuFailure.title: Possible power supply failure
body: The motherboard does not appear to be receiving standby power.
Test with a known-good power supply if available.
Replace the PSU if confirmed faulty.

testPowerButton.title: Check power button operation
body: Press the power button and check whether it feels normal.
A disconnected or failed power switch can prevent startup.

repairPowerButton.title: Repair power button
body: The power button assembly may be disconnected or defective.
Inspect front-panel wiring and replace faulty components.

fansOrLights.title: Do fans spin or lights turn on?
body: Some activity indicates power is reaching the system.
The problem may be startup related rather than a complete power failure.

motherboardFailure.title: Possible motherboard or internal hardware fault
body: The computer receives power but still does not start normally.
Further diagnosis may require testing RAM, CPU, motherboard, and power supply components.
Escalate for hardware diagnostics or repair.
